annotations.label: Perfect number

anchor.snippet: a perfect number is a positive integer that is equal to the sum of its positive proper divisors

annotations.note: `Nat.Perfect n` is defined as `∑ i ∈ properDivisors n, i = n ∧ 0 < n`, exactly the article's definition.

annotations.label: 6 is perfect

anchor.snippet: For instance, 6 has proper divisors 1, 2, and 3

annotations.note: No explicit lemma proving `Nat.Perfect 6` exists in Mathlib, though it is `decide`-able from the definition.

annotations.label: 28 is perfect

anchor.snippet: The next perfect number is 28, because 28 has proper divisors

annotations.note: No explicit lemma proving `Nat.Perfect 28` exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First seven perfect numbers

anchor.snippet: The first seven perfect numbers are

annotations.note: Mathlib has no enumeration or list of specific perfect numbers.

annotations.label: Aliquot sum

anchor.snippet: The sum of proper divisors of a number is called its aliquot sum

annotations.note: The aliquot sum `∑ i ∈ Nat.properDivisors n, i` is expressible via `properDivisors`, but Mathlib has no dedicated named `aliquotSum` function.

annotations.label: Half-sum-of-divisors characterization

anchor.snippet: a perfect number is a number that is half the sum of all of its positive divisors

annotations.note: `Nat.perfect_iff_sum_divisors_eq_two_mul` states `Perfect n ↔ ∑ i ∈ divisors n, i = 2 * n`, i.e. n is half the sum of its divisors.

annotations.label: Nicomachus's claim on perfect number form

anchor.snippet: Nicomachus states without proof that every perfect number is of the form

annotations.note: Only the even case (Euclid–Euler, `even_and_perfect_iff`) is proven; the full claim covering all perfect numbers remains open and is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cataldi: perfect numbers from Euclid's rule end in 6 or 8

anchor.snippet: every perfect number obtained from Euclid's rule ends with a 6 or an 8

annotations.note: No result about the last digit of Euclid-rule perfect numbers exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclid's even perfect number theorem

anchor.snippet: Euclid proved that

annotations.note: `perfect_two_pow_mul_mersenne_of_prime` proves that `(mersenne (k+1)).Prime → Perfect (2^k * mersenne (k+1))`, Euclid's theorem (in the Archive).

annotations.label: First four perfect numbers from formula

anchor.snippet: the first four perfect numbers are generated by the formula

annotations.note: Mathlib does not compute specific perfect numbers from the Euclid formula.

anchors.snippet: the first four perfect numbers are generated by the formula

annotations.label: Mersenne prime

anchor.snippet: are known as Mersenne primes

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the Mersenne number `mersenne p = 2^p - 1` and uses `(mersenne p).Prime`, but has no dedicated `MersennePrime` predicate.

annotations.label: Necessary condition: exponent must be prime

anchor.snippet: it is necessary that p itself be prime

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime.of_mersenne` proves `(mersenne p).Prime → Nat.Prime p`; generalized by `Nat.prime_of_pow_sub_one_prime`.

annotations.label: 2047 is not prime

anchor.snippet: 2047 = 23 × 89 is not a prime number

annotations.note: No explicit lemma about 2047 = 23 × 89 exists, though it is `norm_num`-decidable.

annotations.label: Euclid–Euler theorem

anchor.snippet: indeed yields all the even perfect numbers

annotations.note: `even_and_perfect_iff` is exactly the Euclid–Euler characterization of even perfect numbers (in the Archive).

annotations.label: First 50 even perfect numbers

anchor.snippet: the first 50 even perfect numbers are

annotations.note: Mathlib does not enumerate the first 50 even perfect numbers.

annotations.label: Even perfect numbers are triangular and hexagonal

anchor.snippet: and hence equal to the sum of the integers from 1 to

annotations.note: No result that even perfect numbers are triangular or hexagonal exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Centered nonagonal and sum of odd cubes

anchor.snippet: each even perfect number except for 6 is the

annotations.note: No result connecting even perfect numbers to centered nonagonal numbers or sums of odd cubes exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Triangular form of even perfect numbers (except 6)

anchor.snippet: Even perfect numbers (except 6) are of the form

annotations.note: No such triangular-form result for even perfect numbers exists in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Even perfect numbers (except 6) are of the form

annotations.label: Digital root of even perfect numbers is 1

anchor.snippet: always produces the number 1

annotations.note: No result about the digital root of even perfect numbers exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary representation of even perfect numbers

anchor.snippet: represented in binary form as p ones followed by p − 1 zeros

annotations.note: No result about the binary digit pattern of even perfect numbers exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Even perfect numbers are pernicious

anchor.snippet: every even perfect number is a pernicious number

annotations.note: Pernicious numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Even perfect numbers are practical

anchor.snippet: Every even perfect number is also a practical number

annotations.note: Practical numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pomerance heuristic against odd perfect numbers

anchor.snippet: has presented a heuristic argument suggesting that indeed no odd perfect number should exist

annotations.note: Heuristic arguments about odd perfect numbers are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perfect numbers are harmonic divisor numbers

anchor.snippet: All perfect numbers are also harmonic divisor numbers

annotations.note: Harmonic divisor (Ore) numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Necessary conditions on odd perfect numbers

anchor.snippet: Any odd perfect number N must satisfy the following conditions

annotations.note: No constraints on hypothetical odd perfect numbers are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euler's form of an odd perfect number

anchor.snippet: q , p 1 , ..., p k are distinct odd primes (Euler)

annotations.note: Euler's structural form for odd perfect numbers is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Not all exponents ≡ 1 (mod 3)

anchor.snippet: Not all e i ≡ 1 ( mod 3)

annotations.note: No exponent-congruence constraints on odd perfect numbers exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Not all exponents ≡ 2 (mod 5)

anchor.snippet: Not all e i ≡ 2 ( mod 5)

annotations.note: No exponent-congruence constraints on odd perfect numbers exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Smallest prime factor bound under exponent congruences

anchor.snippet: the smallest prime factor of N must lie between 10 8 and 10 1000

annotations.note: No bounds on the smallest prime factor of odd perfect numbers are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized smallest-prime-factor bound

anchor.snippet: must be smaller than an effectively computable constant depending only on S

annotations.note: This generalized bound on odd perfect numbers is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bound for exponent pattern of ones and twos

anchor.snippet: with t ones and u twos

annotations.note: No bounds on odd-perfect-number exponent patterns are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Excluded exponent tuples
annotations.note: No excluded exponent-tuple results for odd perfect numbers exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Excluded equal exponents

anchor.snippet: then e cannot be 3

annotations.note: No such excluded-exponent results for odd perfect numbers exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Descartes's near-perfect number

anchor.snippet: would be an odd perfect number if only 22021

annotations.note: Descartes's near-perfect number example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Descartes numbers

anchor.snippet: are the Descartes numbers

annotations.note: Descartes numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Makowski: only n³+1 perfect number is 28

anchor.snippet: The only even perfect number of the form n 3 + 1 is 28

annotations.note: Makowski's result is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gallardo: 28 is unique sum of two cubes

anchor.snippet: the only even perfect number that is a sum of two positive cubes of integers

annotations.note: Gallardo's result is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reciprocals of divisors sum to 2

anchor.snippet: The reciprocals of the divisors of a perfect number N must add up to 2

annotations.note: No lemma about the sum of reciprocals of divisors of a perfect number exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Number of divisors is even

anchor.snippet: The number of divisors of a perfect number (whether even or odd) must be even

annotations.note: No result that a perfect number's divisor count is even exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perfect numbers are Ore harmonic numbers

anchor.snippet: every perfect number is an Ore's harmonic number

annotations.note: Ore harmonic numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Even perfect numbers are not trapezoidal

anchor.snippet: The even perfect numbers are not trapezoidal numbers

annotations.note: Trapezoidal numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Three types of non-trapezoidal numbers

anchor.snippet: There are only three types of non-trapezoidal numbers

annotations.note: Trapezoidal numbers and their classification are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Density of perfect numbers

anchor.snippet: The number of perfect numbers less than n is less than

annotations.note: No density/counting bound for perfect numbers is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Even perfect numbers end in 6 or 28

anchor.snippet: Every even perfect number ends in 6 or 28 in base ten

annotations.note: No result about the base-ten ending digits of even perfect numbers exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Only square-free perfect number is 6

anchor.snippet: The only square-free perfect number is 6

annotations.note: No result that 6 is the unique squarefree perfect number exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Deficient and abundant numbers

anchor.snippet: are called deficient , and where it is greater than the number, abundant

annotations.note: `Nat.Deficient` and `Nat.Abundant` are defined via the sum of proper divisors being less than / greater than n.

annotations.label: Amicable and sociable numbers

anchor.snippet: are called amicable , and larger cycles of numbers are called sociable

annotations.note: Amicable and sociable numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Practical number

anchor.snippet: every smaller positive integer is a sum of distinct divisors of it

annotations.note: Practical numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Perfect number as fixed point of restricted divisor function

anchor.snippet: a perfect number is a fixed point of the restricted divisor function

annotations.note: `Nat.perfect_iff_sum_properDivisors` states `Perfect n ↔ ∑ i ∈ properDivisors n, i = n`, the fixed-point condition s(n) = n, though the function s is unnamed.

annotations.label: Granville numbers

anchor.snippet: or Granville numbers

annotations.note: Granville numbers are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Semiperfect number

anchor.snippet: A semiperfect number is a natural number that is equal to the sum of all or some of its proper divisors

annotations.note: `Nat.Pseudoperfect` (synonym for semiperfect) is `0 < n ∧ ∃ s ⊆ properDivisors n, ∑ i ∈ s, i = n`.

annotations.label: Weird numbers

anchor.snippet: abundant numbers which are not semiperfect are called weird numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.Weird n` is defined as `Abundant n ∧ ¬ Pseudoperfect n`, exactly abundant but not semiperfect.
